name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَيَسْـَٔلُونَكَ عَن ذِى ٱلْقَرْنَيْنِ ۖ قُلْ سَأَتْلُوا۟ عَلَيْكُم مِّنْهُ ذِكْرًا (٨٣)
```

```english
They question you concerning Dhul Qarnayn. Say, ‘I will relate to you an account of him.’ (83)
```

```arabic
إِنَّا مَكَّنَّا لَهُۥ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ مِن كُلِّ شَىْءٍ سَبَبًا (٨٤)
```

```english
Indeed We had granted him power in the land and given him the means to all things. (84)
```

```arabic
فَأَتْبَعَ سَبَبًا (٨٥)
```

```english
So he directed a means. (85)
```

```arabic
حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا بَلَغَ مَغْرِبَ ٱلشَّمْسِ وَجَدَهَا تَغْرُبُ فِى عَيْنٍ حَمِئَةٍ وَوَجَدَ عِندَهَا قَوْمًا ۗ قُلْنَا يَـٰذَا ٱلْقَرْنَيْنِ إِمَّآ أَن تُعَذِّبَ وَإِمَّآ أَن تَتَّخِذَ فِيهِمْ حُسْنًا (٨٦)
```

```english
When he reached the place where the sun sets, he found it setting over a warm sea, and by it he found a people. We said, ‘O Dhul Qarnayn! You will either punish them, or treat them with kindness.’ (86)
```

```arabic
قَالَ أَمَّا مَن ظَلَمَ فَسَوْفَ نُعَذِّبُهُۥ ثُمَّ يُرَدُّ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِۦ فَيُعَذِّبُهُۥ عَذَابًا نُّكْرًا (٨٧)
```

```english
He said, ‘As for him who is a wrongdoer, we will punish him. Then he shall be returned to his Lord and He will punish him with a dire punishment. (87)
```

```arabic
وَأَمَّا مَنْ ءَامَنَ وَعَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا فَلَهُۥ جَزَآءً ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ ۖ وَسَنَقُولُ لَهُۥ مِنْ أَمْرِنَا يُسْرًا (٨٨)
```

```english
But as for him who has faith and acts righteously, he shall have the best reward, and we will assign him easy tasks under our command.’ (88)
```

```arabic
ثُمَّ أَتْبَعَ سَبَبًا (٨٩)
```

```english
Thereafter he directed another means. (89)
```

```arabic
حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا بَلَغَ مَطْلِعَ ٱلشَّمْسِ وَجَدَهَا تَطْلُعُ عَلَىٰ قَوْمٍ لَّمْ نَجْعَل لَّهُم مِّن دُونِهَا سِتْرًا (٩٠)
```

```english
When he reached the place where the sun rises, he found it rising on a people for whom We had not provided any shield against it. (90)
```

```arabic
كَذَٰلِكَ وَقَدْ أَحَطْنَا بِمَا لَدَيْهِ خُبْرًا (٩١)
```

```english
So it was, and We were fully aware of whatever [means] he possessed. (91)
```

```arabic
ثُمَّ أَتْبَعَ سَبَبًا (٩٢)
```

```english
Thereafter he directed another means. (92)
```

```arabic
حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا بَلَغَ بَيْنَ ٱلسَّدَّيْنِ وَجَدَ مِن دُونِهِمَا قَوْمًا لَّا يَكَادُونَ يَفْقَهُونَ قَوْلًا (٩٣)
```

```english
When he reached [the place] between the two barriers, he found between them a people who could hardly understand a word [of his language]. (93)
```

```arabic
قَالُوا۟ يَـٰذَا ٱلْقَرْنَيْنِ إِنَّ يَأْجُوجَ وَمَأْجُوجَ مُفْسِدُونَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَهَلْ نَجْعَلُ لَكَ خَرْجًا عَلَىٰٓ أَن تَجْعَلَ بَيْنَنَا وَبَيْنَهُمْ سَدًّا (٩٤)
```

```english
They said, ‘O Dhul Qarnayn! Indeed Gog and Magog are causing disaster in this land. Shall we pay you a tribute on condition that you build a barrier between them and us?’ (94)
```

```arabic
قَالَ مَا مَكَّنِّى فِيهِ رَبِّى خَيْرٌ فَأَعِينُونِى بِقُوَّةٍ أَجْعَلْ بَيْنَكُمْ وَبَيْنَهُمْ رَدْمًا (٩٥)
```

```english
He said, ‘What my Lord has furnished me is better. Yet help me with some strength, and I will make a bulwark between you and them. (95)
```

```arabic
ءَاتُونِى زُبَرَ ٱلْحَدِيدِ ۖ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا سَاوَىٰ بَيْنَ ٱلصَّدَفَيْنِ قَالَ ٱنفُخُوا۟ ۖ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا جَعَلَهُۥ نَارًا قَالَ ءَاتُونِىٓ أُفْرِغْ عَلَيْهِ قِطْرًا (٩٦)
```

```english
Bring me pieces of iron!’ When he had levelled up between the flanks, he said, ‘Blow!’ When he had turned it into fire, he said, ‘Bring me molten copper to pour over it.’ (96)
```

```arabic
فَمَا ٱسْطَـٰعُوٓا۟ أَن يَظْهَرُوهُ وَمَا ٱسْتَطَـٰعُوا۟ لَهُۥ نَقْبًا (٩٧)
```

```english
So they could neither scale it, nor could they make a hole in it. (97)
```

```arabic
قَالَ هَـٰذَا رَحْمَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّى ۖ فَإِذَا جَآءَ وَعْدُ رَبِّى جَعَلَهُۥ دَكَّآءَ ۖ وَكَانَ وَعْدُ رَبِّى حَقًّا (٩٨)
```

```english
He said, ‘This is a mercy from my Lord. But when the promise of my Lord is fulfilled, He will level it; and my Lord’s promise is true.’ (98)
```

```arabic
۞ وَتَرَكْنَا بَعْضَهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَمُوجُ فِى بَعْضٍ ۖ وَنُفِخَ فِى ٱلصُّورِ فَجَمَعْنَـٰهُمْ جَمْعًا (٩٩)
```

```english
That day We shall let them surge over one another, the Trumpet will be blown, and We shall gather them all, (99)
```

```arabic
وَعَرَضْنَا جَهَنَّمَ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْكَـٰفِرِينَ عَرْضًا (١٠٠)
```

```english
and on that day We shall bring hell into view visibly for the faithless. (100)
```

```arabic
ٱلَّذِينَ كَانَتْ أَعْيُنُهُمْ فِى غِطَآءٍ عَن ذِكْرِى وَكَانُوا۟ لَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ سَمْعًا (١٠١)
```

```english
—Those whose eyes were blind to My remembrance and who could not hear. (101)
```

```arabic
أَفَحَسِبَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ أَن يَتَّخِذُوا۟ عِبَادِى مِن دُونِىٓ أَوْلِيَآءَ ۚ إِنَّآ أَعْتَدْنَا جَهَنَّمَ لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ نُزُلًا (١٠٢)
```

```english
Do the faithless suppose that they have taken My servants for guardians in My stead? Indeed, We have prepared hell for the hospitality of the faithless. (102)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses contain the story of *Dhū al-Qarnayn*, and within them are some of the prophecies (ملاحم) of the Quran:

**His saying, the Exalted:** «وَيَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنْ ذِي الْقَرْنَيْنِ قُلْ سَأَتْلُوا عَلَيْكُمْ مِنْهُ ذِكْراً» — *They ask you about Dhū al-Qarnayn. Say, ‘I will let you into something of his story.’* (18:83). That is, they ask you about the state and affairs of *Dhū al-Qarnayn*. The evidence for this is that His answer to the question was by mentioning his affairs, not by defining his person, to the extent that He sufficed with his title and did not go beyond it to mention his name.

The word "remembrance" (الذكر) is either a verbal noun (مصدر) used in the sense of the object (مفعول), meaning: "Say, I shall recite to you from him—meaning from *Dhū al-Qarnayn*—something mentioned (مذكوراً)"; or what is meant by "remembrance" is the Quran—as Allah has named it "the Remembrance" (الذكر) in several places of His speech—and the meaning would be: "Say, I shall recite to you from him—meaning regarding *Dhū al-Qarnayn*—or from Allah, a Quranic recitation," which is what follows this verse from His saying: «إِنَّا مَكَّنَّا لَهُ» — *Indeed We established him...* until the end of the story. The second meaning is more apparent.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «إِنَّا مَكَّنَّا لَهُ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَآتَيْناهُ مِنْ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ سَبَباً» — *Indeed We established him in the land and gave him the means to all things.* (18:84). **Establishment** (التمكين) means the granting of power (الإقدار). It is said: "I established him" (مكنته) or "I established for him" (مكنت له), meaning I gave him power. Thus, establishment in the earth is the power to dispose of it through sovereignty however he wished and intended. It is sometimes said that it is a verbal noun derived from "place" (المكان) based on the assumption that the letter *mīm* is original; thus, establishment is the granting of stability and firmness such that no competing obstacle can remove him from his place.

And **the means** (السبب) refers to a connection (الوصلة) or a medium (الوسيلة). Thus, the meaning of giving him a means to all things is that he was given, from everything that leads to important and vital objectives, that which he could use and benefit from, such as intellect (العقل), knowledge (العلم), religion (الدين), physical strength (قوة الجسم), abundance of wealth (كثرة المال) and soldiers (الجند), vastness of dominion (سعة الملك), sound management (حسن التدبير), and other such things. This is a favor from Allah, the Exalted, upon *Dhū al-Qarnayn* and an aggrandizement of his affair in the most eloquent manner. What Allah has narrated of his conduct, actions, and speech—filled with wisdom and power—testifies to this.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «فَأَتْبَعَ سَبَباً» — *So he followed a course.* (18:85). **Following** (الإتباع) means to pursue or catch up; that is, he pursued a means and took a connection and a medium by which he traveled toward the setting of the sun.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «حَتَّى إِذا بَلَغَ مَغْرِبَ الشَّمْسِ وَجَدَها تَغْرُبُ فِي عَيْنٍ حَمِئَةٍ وَوَجَدَ عِنْدَها قَوْماً» — *until when he reached the place where the sun sets, he found it setting in a muddy spring, and he found a people beside it.* (18:86). The word "until" (حَتَّى) indicates a suppressed verb, the estimation of which is: "So he traveled until when he reached..." What is meant by the "place where the sun sets" (مغرب الشمس) is the end of the inhabited world at that time from the western side, as evidenced by His saying: "and he found a people beside it."

They have mentioned that what is meant by the "muddy spring" (العين الحمئة) is a spring possessing black mud (الحمأة), and that "spring" (العين) may refer to the sea, as it is sometimes applied to it. The meaning of finding the sun setting in a muddy spring is that he stood on the shore of a sea beyond which there was no hope of finding land, so he saw the sun as if it were setting into the sea due to the horizon meeting it. It has been said that this muddy spring corresponds to the Atlantic Ocean (المحيط الغربي), which contained the "Eternal Isles" (الجزائر الخالدات) that were previously the starting point of longitude before they were submerged.

It has also been recited as "in a hot spring" (عَيْنٍ حامِيَةٍ), meaning a warm one. This corresponds to points near the equator in the Atlantic Ocean adjacent to Africa. Perhaps *Dhū al-Qarnayn*, in his western journey, reached the coasts of Africa.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «قُلْنا يا ذَا الْقَرْنَيْنِ إِمَّا أَنْ تُعَذِّبَ وَإِمَّا أَنْ تَتَّخِذَ فِيهِمْ حُسْناً» — *We said, ‘O Dhū al-Qarnayn! Either you shall punish them, or you shall treat them with kindness.’* (18:86). Speech attributed to Allah, the Exalted, in the Quran is used for prophetic revelation (الوحي النبوي) and for communication through revelation, such as His saying: «وَقُلْنا يا آدَمُ اسْكُنْ» — *And We said, ‘O Adam, dwell...’* (2:35) and His saying: «وَإِذْ قُلْنَا ادْخُلُوا هذِهِ الْقَرْيَةَ» — *And when We said, ‘Enter this town...’* (2:58). It is also used for inspiration (الإلهام) that is not of prophecy, such as His saying: «وَأَوْحَيْنا إِلى أُمِّ مُوسى أَنْ أَرْضِعِيهِ» — *And We revealed to the mother of Moses, ‘Suckle him...’* (28:7).

From this, it becomes clear that His saying: "We said, O Dhū al-Qarnayn..." etc., does not definitively prove that he was a prophet to whom revelation was sent, because Allah's speech is broader than the revelation specific to prophecy. His saying: «ثُمَّ يُرَدُّ إِلى رَبِّهِ فَيُعَذِّبُهُ» — *then he will be returned to his Lord, and He will punish him...* (18:87), where it is mentioned in the third-person context (الغيبة) regarding Allah, the Exalted, is not devoid of an indication that his conversation was through a prophet who was with him. Thus, his kingship would be similar to the kingship of *Ṭālūt* (Saul) among the Children of Israel, by the indication and guidance of their prophet.

His saying: «إِمَّا أَنْ تُعَذِّبَ وَإِمَّا أَنْ تَتَّخِذَ فِيهِمْ حُسْناً» — *Either you shall punish them, or you shall treat them with kindness*—means: either you punish these people or you adopt among them a matter characterized by goodness (حُسْناً). Thus, "goodness" (حُسْناً) is a verbal noun used in the sense of the active participle, standing in place of its described noun, or it is a description for emphasis. It has been said that contrasting punishment with "adopting goodness" hints at its preference. The speech is a declarative alternation intended for permission (الإباحة); thus, it is a performative (إنشاء) in the form of a report. The meaning is: "You have the right to punish them and you have the right to pardon them," as has been said. However, it is more apparent that it is an inquiry into what he will do with them—whether policy or pardon—which is more consistent with the context of the answer that includes the detail of punishment and kindness: «أَمَّا مَنْ ظَلَمَ فَسَوْفَ نُعَذِّبُهُ» — *As for him who is a wrongdoer, we shall punish him...* (18:87). For if His saying: "Either you shall punish..." etc., were a choice-based ruling, then His saying: "As for him who is a wrongdoer..." etc., would be a confirmation of it and an announcement of acceptance, which would not carry much benefit.

The sum of the meaning is: We inquired of him what he intended to do with them in terms of punishment or kindness, now that he had overcome them and gained authority over them. He replied: We shall punish the wrongdoer among them, then he will be returned to his Lord, who will punish him with a terrible punishment; and we shall be kind to the righteous believer and charge him with that which is easy.

The object was not mentioned in His saying: "Either you shall punish," unlike His saying: "or you shall treat them with kindness," because not all of them were wrongdoers, and it is not permissible to generalize punishment for a people in such a state, unlike generalizing kindness for a people among whom are both the righteous and the wicked.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «أَمَّا مَنْ ظَلَمَ فَسَوْفَ نُعَذِّبُهُ ثُمَّ يُرَدُّ إِلى رَبِّهِ فَيُعَذِّبُهُ عَذاباً نُكْراً» — *As for him who is a wrongdoer, we shall punish him, then he will be returned to his Lord, and He will punish him with a terrible punishment.* (18:87). **Terrible** (النكر والمنكر) means that which is unfamiliar; that is, He will punish him with a punishment the likes of which he has never known, nor did he expect or anticipate.

Wrongdoing (الظلم) has been interpreted as polytheism (الإشراك), and punishment (التعذيب) as killing. Thus, the meaning of "As for him who is a wrongdoer, we shall punish him" would be: "As for him who associates partners with Allah and does not turn back from his polytheism, we shall kill him." This seems to be taken from the contrast of "him who is a wrongdoer" with His saying: "him who has faith and acts righteously." However, it is apparent from the contrast that what is meant by the "wrongdoer" is broader than one who commits polytheism and does not believe in Allah, or one who believes and does not commit polytheism but spreads corruption in the land. Were it not for the restriction of its opposite by "faith," the apparent meaning of wrongdoing would be corruption (الإفساد) without regard to polytheism, because it is the custom of kings, when they are just, to purge their land of the corruption of the corrupters. Likewise, there is no evidence for restricting punishment to killing.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «وَأَمَّا مَنْ آمَنَ وَعَمِلَ صالِحاً فَلَهُ جَزاءً الْحُسْنى» — *But as for him who has faith and acts righteously, he shall have the best reward...* (18:88). "Righteously" (صالِحاً) is an adjective standing in place of its described noun, and likewise "the best" (الحسنى). "Reward" (جَزاءً) is a circumstantial qualifier (حال), a specification (تمييز), or a cognate accusative (مفعول مطلق). The estimation is: "As for him who believes and performs a righteous deed, he shall have the best recompense while being rewarded, or in terms of reward, or We shall reward him a reward."

And His saying: «وَسَنَقُولُ لَهُ مِنْ أَمْرِنا يُسْراً» — *and we will give him an easy command.* (18:88). **Ease** (اليسر) means that which is made easy (الميسور); it is an adjective standing in place of its described noun. It is apparent that what is meant by "command" (الأمر) is the legislative command (الأمر التكليفي). The estimation of the speech is: "And We shall speak to him an easy word regarding Our command," meaning We shall charge him with what is easy for him and not difficult upon him.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «ثُمَّ أَتْبَعَ سَبَباً حَتَّى إِذا بَلَغَ مَطْلِعَ الشَّمْسِ» — *Then he followed a course until when he reached the place where the sun rises...* (18:89-90). That is, he then prepared a means for travel and journeyed toward the East until he reached the desert on the eastern side and found the sun rising upon a nomadic people for whom We had not provided any shelter against it.

What is meant by "shelter" (الستر) is that which one uses to shield oneself from the sun, namely buildings and clothing, or specifically buildings. That is, they lived on the open ground without having houses to take refuge in and shield themselves from the sun, and they were naked without clothing upon them. The attribution of this to Allah, the Glorified, in His saying: "We had not provided for them..." etc., is an indication that they had not yet become aware of that, nor had they learned the construction of houses, the taking of tents, or the weaving and sewing of garments.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «كَذلِكَ وَقَدْ أَحَطْنا بِما لَدَيْهِ خُبْراً» — *So it was, and We had full knowledge of what he had with him.* (18:91). It is apparent that His saying: "So it was" (كَذلِكَ) refers to their aforementioned description. Comparing a thing to itself based on a claim of distinction provides a type of emphasis. Other views have been mentioned regarding what is referred to by "that," but they are far-fetched.

And His saying: «وَقَدْ أَحَطْنا بِما لَدَيْهِ خُبْراً» — *and We had full knowledge of what he had with him.* The pronoun refers to *Dhū al-Qarnayn*, and the sentence is a circumstantial clause (جملة حالية). The meaning is that he took the means of travel and reached the rising place of the sun and found a people such-and-such, in a state where Our knowledge and expertise encompassed what he possessed of equipment and preparation, and what he was carrying out or what was happening to him. It is apparent that the encompassment of His knowledge, the Exalted, of what he had is a metaphor for the fact that what he chose and performed was by guidance from Allah and His command. Thus, he did not proceed or act except based on a guidance he followed and a command he obeyed, as He indicated a similar meaning when mentioning his journey to the West by His saying: "We said, O Dhū al-Qarnayn..." etc.

Thus, the verse—meaning His saying: "and We had full knowledge..." etc.—in its metaphorical meaning is similar to His saying: «وَاصْنَعِ الْفُلْكَ بِأَعْيُنِنا وَوَحْيِنا» — *Build the ark before Our eyes and by Our revelation...* (11:37), and His saying: «أَنْزَلَهُ بِعِلْمِهِ» — *He has sent it down with His knowledge...* (4:166), and His saying: «وَأَحاطَ بِما لَدَيْهِمْ» — *and He has encompassed what is with them...* (72:28).

It has been said that the verse is to convey the greatness of his affair and that no one encompasses its subtleties and details except Allah; or to emphasize the magnitude of what *Dhū al-Qarnayn* endured in this journey and that the hardships and difficulties he bore were within the knowledge of Allah and not hidden from Him; or to aggrandize the "means" (السبب) that he followed. What we have presented is more appropriate.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «ثُمَّ أَتْبَعَ سَبَباً حَتَّى إِذا بَلَغَ بَيْنَ السَّدَّيْنِ» — *Then he followed a course until when he reached [a place] between the two barriers...* (18:92-93). **The barrier** (السد) is the mountain and every obstacle that blocks the path of passage. It seems what is meant by them is two mountains. And His saying: «وَجَدَ مِنْ دُونِهِما قَوْماً» — *he found a people beside them*—means near them. And His saying: «لا يَكادُونَ يَفْقَهُونَ قَوْلاً» — *who could barely understand any speech*—is a metaphor for their simplicity and the naivety of their understanding. It is sometimes said to be a metaphor for the strangeness of their language and its distance from the languages known to them, but this is not without far-fetchedness.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «قالُوا يا ذَا الْقَرْنَيْنِ إِنَّ يَأْجُوجَ وَمَأْجُوجَ مُفْسِدُونَ» — *They said, ‘O Dhū al-Qarnayn! Indeed Gog and Magog are cause of corruption...’* (18:94). It is apparent that the speakers are the people he found beside the two mountains. **Gog and Magog** (يأجوج ومأجوج) were two races of people who used to come to them from beyond the two mountains, raiding them and overwhelming them with killing, captivity, and looting. The evidence for this is the context, including the pronouns for rational beings, the construction of the barrier between the two mountains, and other details.

And His saying: «فَهَلْ نَجْعَلُ لَكَ خَرْجاً» — *‘So shall we pay you a tribute...’* **Tribute** (الخرج) is what is paid out of wealth to be spent on some needs. They offered to give him wealth on the condition that he place a barrier between them and Gog and Magog to prevent their transgression and aggression against them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «قالَ ما مَكَّنِّي فِيهِ رَبِّي خَيْرٌ فَأَعِينُونِي بِقُوَّةٍ أَجْعَلْ بَيْنَكُمْ وَبَيْنَهُمْ رَدْماً» — *He said, ‘What my Lord has established me in is better. So help me with power, and I will set up a rampart between you and them.’* (18:95). The origin of "established me" (مَكَّنِّي) is *makkannī*, then one of the two *nūns* was assimilated into the other. **The rampart** (الردم) is the barrier (السد), and it is said to be a strong barrier. Based on this, the use of the term "rampart" in the response, when they had asked for a "barrier," is an answer and a promise of something superior to what they had requested and hoped for.

And His saying: «قالَ ما مَكَّنِّي فِيهِ رَبِّي خَيْرٌ» — *He said, ‘What my Lord has established me in is better’*—is an expression of *Dhū al-Qarnayn's* lack of need for the tribute they offered him in exchange for making the barrier. He says: "The abundance and power in which my Lord has established and settled me is better than the wealth you promise me, so I have no need for it."

And His saying: «فأَعِينُونِي بِقُوَّةٍ» — *So help me with power...* etc. **Power** (القوة) is that by which one is strengthened for a task. The sentence is a derivation from what resulted from their offer, which was their request for him to make a barrier for them. The sum of the meaning is: "As for the tribute, I have no need for it. As for the barrier, if you desire it, then help me with what will strengthen me in its construction, such as men and what is used in its building"—and he mentioned among them pieces of iron, molten copper, and blowing with bellows—"and I shall make for you a strong barrier."

By this meaning, it becomes clear that what they meant by the tribute they offered was the wage for the work of the barrier.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «آتُونِي زُبَرَ الْحَدِيدِ» — *‘Bring me ingots of iron...’* (18:96). **Ingots** (الزبر) is the plural of *zubrah*, which is a piece. And "leveled" (ساوى) means made equal (سوى) according to what has been said, and it has been recited as *suwwiya*. And **the two cliffs** (الصَّدَفَيْنِ) is the dual of *ṣadaf*, which is one of the two sides of a mountain. Some have mentioned that it is only said when there is another mountain parallel to it on its side; thus, it is among the relative nouns (الأسماء المتضائفة) like "spouse" and "double." And **molten copper** (القطر) is copper or brass that has been melted, and "pouring it" means casting it over the holes, defects, and gaps.

And His saying: «آتُونِي زُبَرَ الْحَدِيدِ» — *‘Bring me ingots of iron’*—means give them to me so that I may use them in the barrier, and it is part of the "power" with which he sought their help. Perhaps he specified it and did not mention stones and other building requirements because it is the pillar in the fortification of the barrier's construction. Thus, the sentence "Bring me ingots of iron" is a substitution of a part for the whole (بدل البعض من الكل) from the sentence "So help me with power," or the speech assumes a suppressed "he said," which is frequent in the Quran.

And His saying: «حَتَّى إِذا ساوى بَيْنَ الصَّدَفَيْنِ قالَ انْفُخُوا» — *until when he had leveled [the gap] between the two cliffs, he said, ‘Blow!’* (18:96). In the speech is a conciseness through omission (إيجاز بالحذف), and the estimation is: "So they helped him with power and brought him what he requested of them, and he built the barrier for them and raised it until when he had leveled between the two cliffs, he said: Blow!"

And His saying: «قالَ انْفُخُوا» — *he said, ‘Blow!’*—it is apparent that it omits the object of the verb to indicate the action itself. What is meant is the setting up of bellows upon the barrier to heat the iron placed within it and to pour the molten copper over its gaps and openings.

And His saying: «حَتَّى إِذا جَعَلَهُ ناراً قالَ» — *until when he had made it [red as] fire, he said...* (18:96). In the speech is omission and conciseness. The estimation is: "So he blew until when he made it—meaning that which was blown into, or the iron—fire, meaning like fire in its appearance and heat; thus, it is a metaphor."

And His saying: «قالَ آتُونِي أُفْرِغْ عَلَيْهِ قِطْراً» — *he said, ‘Bring me molten copper to pour over it.’* (18:96). That is, bring me molten copper that I may pour and cast it over it to seal its gaps, whereby the barrier becomes solid and no penetrator can penetrate it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «فَمَا اسْطاعُوا أَنْ يَظْهَرُوهُ وَمَا اسْتَطاعُوا لَهُ نَقْباً» — *So they could not scale it, nor could they dig a hole through it.* (18:97). *Isṭā‘ū* and *istaṭā‘ū* are the same. **Scaling** (الظهور) is ascending and overcoming, and **digging a hole** (النقب) is piercing. Al-Rāghib said in *al-Mufradāt*: "A hole (النقب) in a wall or skin is like a hole (الثقب) in wood." End quote. The plural pronouns refer to Gog and Magog. In the speech is omission and conciseness. The estimation is: "So he built the barrier, and Gog and Magog could not ascend it due to its height, nor could they pierce it due to its fortification."

**His saying, the Exalted:** «قالَ هذا رَحْمَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّي فَإِذا جاءَ وَعْدُ رَبِّي جَعَلَهُ دَكَّاءَ وَكانَ وَعْدُ رَبِّي حَقًّا» — *He said, ‘This is a mercy from my Lord. But when the promise of my Lord comes to pass, He will level it, and the promise of my Lord is true.’* (18:98). **Leveling** (الدكاء) is crushing (الدك), which is the most intense pounding; it is a verbal noun used in the sense of the passive participle. It has been said that what is meant is a "level camel" (الناقة الدكاء), which is one without a hump; based on this, it is a metaphor, and what is meant is the destruction of the barrier, as they have said.

And His saying: «قالَ هذا رَحْمَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّي» — *He said, ‘This is a mercy from my Lord’*—that is, *Dhū al-Qarnayn* said, after he built the barrier: "This—meaning the barrier—is a mercy from my Lord," meaning a blessing and a protection by which the evil of Gog and Magog is repelled from nations of people.

And His saying: «فَإِذا جاءَ وَعْدُ رَبِّي جَعَلَهُ دَكَّاءَ» — *But when the promise of my Lord comes to pass, He will level it.* In the speech is omission and conciseness. The estimation is: "And this mercy shall remain until the coming of the promise of my Lord, and when the promise of my Lord comes, He shall make it crushed and level it with the ground."

What is meant by the "promise" (الوعد) is either a specific promise from Him, the Exalted, regarding the barrier—that it will be crushed near the approach of the Hour, thus being a prophecy (ملحمة) reported by *Dhū al-Qarnayn*—or His general promise, the Exalted, of the onset of the Hour, which crushes the mountains and destroys the world. He confirmed the statement with the sentence: "and the promise of my Lord is true."

**His saying, the Exalted:** «وَتَرَكْنا بَعْضَهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَمُوجُ فِي بَعْضٍ» — *On that day We will leave them to surge against one another...* (18:99). The apparent context is that the plural pronoun refers to the people, and this is supported by the return of the pronoun in "then We will gather them" (فَجَمَعْناهُمْ) to the people definitively, because the ruling of gathering is general.

In His saying: "some of them on that day surging against others," there is a metaphor. What is meant is that they will be agitated on that day from the intensity of the terror, like the agitation of the sea by the surging of some of it against others. Order will vanish from among them, chaos and confusion will prevail, and their Lord will turn away from them, so His mercy will not encompass them, nor will His care rectify their state.

Thus, the verse is like a detail for the summary contained in the saying of *Dhū al-Qarnayn*: "But when the promise of my Lord comes to pass, He will level it." Similar to it is His saying, the Exalted, in another place: «حَتَّى إِذا فُتِحَتْ يَأْجُوجُ وَمَأْجُوجُ وَهُمْ مِنْ كُلِّ حَدَبٍ يَنْسِلُونَ وَاقْتَرَبَ الْوَعْدُ الْحَقُّ فَإِذا هِيَ شاخِصَةٌ أَبْصارُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا يا وَيْلَنا قَدْ كُنَّا فِي غَفْلَةٍ مِنْ هذا بَلْ كُنَّا ظالِمِينَ» — *until when Gog and Magog are let loose, and they surge down from every elevation. And the true promise draws near: then behold, the eyes of the faithless will be transfixed: ‘Woe to us! We were certainly oblivious of this! Rather we were wrongdoers.’* (21:96-97). In any case, it is among the prophecies.

It has become clear from what has passed that "leaving" (الترك) in the verse carries its primary meaning, which is the opposite of "taking," and there is no reason for what some have mentioned: that "leaving" means "making" and is among the words with opposite meanings (الأضداد). End quote.

The verse is from the speech of Allah, the Glorified, and is not a continuation of the speech of *Dhū al-Qarnayn*. The evidence for this is the change in context from the third person to the first person plural (تكلم مع الغير), which is the context of His previous speech: "Indeed We established him," "We said, O Dhū al-Qarnayn." Had it been a continuation of *Dhū al-Qarnayn's* speech, it would have been said: "and He will leave some of them," in alignment with his saying: "He will level it."

And His saying: «وَنُفِخَ فِي الصُّورِ» — *and the Trumpet will be blown...* (18:99). This is the second blowing in which there is the bringing to life, as evidenced by His saying: «فَجَمَعْناهُمْ جَمْعاً وَعَرَضْنا جَهَنَّمَ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلْكافِرِينَ عَرْضاً» — *and We will gather them all together. On that day We will present hell to the faithless, a [terrible] presentation.* (18:99-100).

**His saying, the Exalted:** «الَّذِينَ كانَتْ أَعْيُنُهُمْ فِي غِطاءٍ عَنْ ذِكْرِي وَكانُوا لا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ سَمْعاً» — *those whose eyes were under a veil from My remembrance, and who could not bear to hear.* (18:101).

This is an explanation of "the faithless" (الكافرين). These are the ones between whom and His remembrance Allah has placed a barrier—and on this occasion, He addressed their state after mentioning the barrier of Gog and Magog—so He placed their eyes under a veil from His remembrance and took away the ability to hear from their ears. Thus, the path between them and the Truth—which is the remembrance of Allah—was cut off.

For the Truth is only attained either through the path of sight, by looking at the signs of Allah, the Glorified, and being guided to what they indicate and lead to; or through the path of hearing, by listening to wisdom, exhortation, stories, and lessons. These people possess neither sight nor hearing.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «أَفَحَسِبَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا أَنْ يَتَّخِذُوا عِبادِي مِنْ دُونِي أَوْلِياءَ» — *Do the faithless suppose that they can take My servants as protectors besides Me?* (18:102). The interrogation is for denial (الإنكار). It is said in *al-Majma‘*: "Its meaning is: Do those who denied the monotheism (التوحيد) of Allah suppose that they can take lords besides Me who will help them and repel My punishment from them?" He said: "And this suppressed part is indicated by His saying: «إِنَّا أَعْتَدْنا جَهَنَّمَ لِلْكافِرِينَ نُزُلاً» — *Indeed We have prepared hell as an abode for the faithless.*" End quote.

There is a second view narrated from Ibn ‘Abbās, which is that the meaning is: "Do those who disbelieve suppose that they can take gods besides Me while I do not become angry for My own sake against them nor punish them?"

A third view: that "that they can take" (أَنْ يَتَّخِذُوا) etc., is the first object of "suppose" (حسب) in the sense of "think" (ظن), and its second object is suppressed. The estimation is: "Do those who disbelieve suppose their taking My servants as protectors besides Me to be beneficial for them or repelling punishment from them?" The difference between this view and the previous two is that "that" (أَنْ) and its relative clause stand in place of both objects in those two, and the suppressed part is part of the relative clause in them, unlike the third view where "that" and its relative clause are the first object of "suppose," and the second object is suppressed.

A fourth view: that "that" and its relative clause stand in place of both objects, and the concern of the speech is directed toward denying that the "taking" is a taking in reality, in the sense that this is not a "taking" at all, since taking only occurs from both sides, and those who are taken [as gods] disassociate themselves from them due to their saying: «سُبْحانَكَ أَنْتَ وَلِيُّنا مِنْ دُونِهِمْ» — *‘Immaculate are You! You are our guardian, not they!’* (34:41).

The four views are arranged in order of merit, and the most appropriate of them is the first. The context of these verses supports it, for these verses—rather, generally the verses of the Surah—are directed toward explaining that they were tempted by the glitter of the life of this world and matters became confused for them, so they felt secure in the outward means and took protectors besides Him, the Exalted.

They think that their guardianship will suffice them, benefit them, and repel harm from them, while the reality is that what they will encounter after the blowing and the gathering contradicts that. Thus, the verse denies them this thought and supposition after their end became such. Furthermore, the possibility of "that" and its relative clause standing in place of the two objects of "suppose" has occurred frequently in His speech, the Exalted, such as His saying: «أَمْ حَسِبَ الَّذِينَ اجْتَرَحُوا السَّيِّئاتِ أَنْ نَجْعَلَهُمْ كَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا» — *Do those who commit misdeeds suppose that We shall treat them like those who have faith...* (45:21) and others, which obviates the need for estimating a suppressed second object, which some grammarians have prohibited.

This is supported by the following verses: «قُلْ هَلْ نُنَبِّئُكُمْ بِالْأَخْسَرِينَ أَعْمالاً» — *Say, ‘Shall we inform you about the biggest losers in regard to their works?’* (18:103) etc., as well as the recitation attributed to ‘Alī (peace be upon him) and a number of others: "Supposes" (أَفَحَسْبُ) with a *sukūn* on the *sīn* and a *ḍammah* on the *bā’*. The meaning would be: "Is the taking of My servants as protectors besides Me sufficient for them?"

What is meant by "servants" in His saying: "that they can take My servants as protectors besides Me" is everyone whom the polytheists (الوثنيون) worship, including angels, jinn, and the perfected among human beings.

As for what the commentators have mentioned—that what is meant by them is the Messiah (peace be upon him), the angels, and their like among those brought near, excluding the devils, because the majority of such phrasing as "My servants" (عِبادِي) is for the addition to be for the honor of the possessed—

In this, firstly, the context does not suit honoring, which is apparent. Secondly, the restriction "besides Me" (مِنْ دُونِي) in the speech is explicit that what is meant by "those who disbelieve" are the polytheists who do not worship Allah despite acknowledging His divinity, but rather worship the partners and intercessors. As for the People of the Book, for example, the Christians in their taking the Messiah as a protector, they do not negate the guardianship of Allah, but rather affirm both guardianships together and then consider them one—so understand that. Thus, the truth is that His saying: "My servants" does not encompass the Messiah and those like him among humans, but is specific to the gods of the polytheists, and what is meant by "those who disbelieve" are the polytheists only.

And His saying: «إِنَّا أَعْتَدْنا جَهَنَّمَ لِلْكافِرِينَ نُزُلاً» — *Indeed We have prepared hell as an abode for the faithless.* (18:102). That is, something they enjoy upon their first arrival in the Abode of the Hereafter. He likened the Abode of the Hereafter to a house where a guest arrives, and Hell to the "abode" (النزل) with which the arriving guest is honored upon his first entry. This comparison is increased in subtlety and beauty by what will come after two verses: that no weight will be assigned to them on the Day of Resurrection; so it is as if they do not tarry before entering the Fire. In the verse is a sarcasm that is not hidden, as if it were a response to what will be narrated of their sarcasm in the world in His saying: «وَاتَّخِذُوا آياتِي وَرُسُلِي هُزُواً» — *and took My signs and My apostles in derision.* (18:106).

## (Narrative Discussion)

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummī: "When the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) informed them of the news of Moses, his young man, and al-Khiḍr, they said to him: 'Inform us about a traveler who circled the earth, the East and the West—who was he? And what is his story?' So Allah sent down: «وَيَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنْ ذِي الْقَرْنَيْنِ» — *They ask you about Dhū al-Qarnayn...* the verses."

**I say:** The detail of this narration has already passed in the discussion on the story of the People of the Cave. Something similar in meaning was also narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthūr* from Ibn Abī Ḥātim from al-Suddī and from ‘Umar, the freed slave of Ghafrah.

Know that the narrations reported through the chains of the Shī‘ah and the Sunnīs from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and through the chains of the Shī‘ah from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام), as well as the views narrated from the Companions and the Successors—which the Sunnīs treat as *mawquf* traditions—regarding the story of *Dhū al-Qarnayn*, are strangely divergent, contradictory, and inconsistent in all the particulars of the story and all its aspects. Moreover, they contain wonders from which sound taste recoils, or which the intellect deems impossible and existence denies. The critical researcher, when comparing some to others and reflecting upon them, will not doubt that they are not free from interpolation, fabrication, and strange exaggerations in describing the story. The strangest of them are what is narrated from the Jewish scholars who embraced Islam, such as Wahb ibn Munabbih and Ka‘b al-Aḥbār, or what the indications suggest is taken from them. In this state, it does not benefit us to narrate them exhaustively despite their abundance and length; rather, we shall only point out some aspects of their differences and restrict ourselves to narrating what is generally free from contradiction.

Among the differences is the difference regarding his nature: most narrations state that he was a human, while it has been reported (1) in some of them that he was a heavenly angel whom Allah sent down to the earth and gave him the means to all things. In the *Khiṭaṭ* of al-Maqrīzī, from al-Jāḥiẓ in the *Book of Animals*, it is said that *Dhū al-Qarnayn's* mother was a human and his father was from the angels.

Among them is the difference in his status: in most narrations, he was a righteous servant who loved Allah, so Allah loved him, and he was sincere toward Allah, so Allah was sincere toward him. In some of them (1), he was a *muḥaddath* (one spoken to by angels) whom the angel would visit and speak to, and in some of them (2), he was a prophet.

Among them is the difference in his name: in some, his name is (3) ‘Ayyāsh; in some, (4) Alexander; in some, (5) Marziyā ibn Marzabah the Greek, from the descendants of Yūnan ibn Yāfith ibn Nūḥ; in some, (6) Muṣ‘ab ibn ‘Abd Allāh from Qaḥṭān; in some, (7) Ṣa‘b ibn Dhī al-Marāthid, the first of the *Tabābi‘ah* (kings of Yemen), and it is as if he were the *Tubba‘* Abū Karib; in some, (8) ‘Abd Allāh ibn Ḍaḥḥāk ibn Ma‘add, and others besides these, which are many.

Among them is the difference in the reason for his being named *Dhū al-Qarnayn* (The Two-Horned One): in some (9), he called his people to Allah, so they struck him on his right horn (side of the head), and he disappeared from them for a time, then he came to them and called them to Allah a second time, so they struck him on his left horn, and he disappeared from them for a time, then Allah gave him the means and he traversed the East and West of the earth, so he was named *Dhū al-Qarnayn*. In some (10), they killed him with the first blow, then Allah brought him back to life, so he came to them and called them, and they struck him and killed him a second time, then Allah brought him back to life and raised him to the lowest heaven, then sent him down to the earth and gave him the means to all things.

In some (1), after the second revival, two horns grew for him in the places of the two wounds, and Allah subjected for him light and darkness. Then, when he descended to the earth, he traveled through it and called to Allah; he would roar like a lion, and his two horns would flash and thunder. If a people were too arrogant to accept his call, he would set darkness upon them until it exhausted them and they were forced to answer it.

In some (2), he had two horns on his head and would wear a turban over them to hide them. He was the first to wear a turban. He used to hide them from the people, and no one knew of it except his scribe. He had forbidden him from informing anyone of it, so the scribe's chest became tight with it. He went to the desert, placed his mouth to the ground, and called out: "Behold, the king has two horns!" Then Allah caused two reeds to grow from his word. A shepherd passed by them, liked them, cut them, and made them into flutes. Whenever he played the flute, there would come out from the two reeds: "Behold, the king has two horns!" This spread in the city, so he sent for the scribe, interrogated him, and threatened him with death if he did not tell the truth. He narrated the story to him, and *Dhū al-Qarnayn* said: "This is a matter Allah wished to manifest," and he removed the turban from his head.

It has been said (3): He was named *Dhū al-Qarnayn* because he ruled the two "horns" of the earth, which are the East and the West. It was said (4): Because he saw in a dream that he took hold of the two horns of the sun, so it was interpreted for him as ruling the East and West, and he was named *Dhū al-Qarnayn*. It was said (5): Because he had two locks of hair (عقيصتان) on his head. It was said (6): Because he ruled Rome and Persia. It was said (7): Because he had something resembling two horns on his head. It was said (8): Because there were two horns of gold on his crown—among other things that have been said.

Among them is the difference regarding his journey to the West and the East, and in this is the most intense disagreement. It has been narrated (1) that the clouds were subjected to him, so he would ride the clouds and travel in the earth West and East. It was narrated (2) that he reached Mount *Qāf*, which is a green mountain encompassing the world, from which comes the greenness of the sky. It was narrated (3) that he sought the Fountain of Life and was directed that it was in the Darknesses, so he entered them, and in his vanguard was al-Khiḍr. *Dhū al-Qarnayn* was not granted to drink from it, but al-Khiḍr drank and bathed in it, so he was granted eternal life. In these narrations, the Darknesses are on the side of the East.

Among them is the difference in the location of the barrier he built: in some, it is in (4) the East, and in some (5), it is in the North. The exaggeration of some narrations reached the point of mentioning (6) that the length of the barrier—which is the distance between the two mountains—is a hundred leagues (فرسخ), its width is fifty leagues, and its height is the height of the two mountains. He dug a foundation for it until he reached water, and he made its filling rocks and its clay copper, then he topped it with ingots of iron and molten copper, and placed between them a vein of yellow copper, so it became like a striped garment.

Among them is the difference in the description of Gog and Magog: it was narrated (7) that they are from the Turks and from the descendants of Yāfith ibn Nūḥ; they used to spread corruption in the land, so the barrier was built against them. It was narrated (8) that they are from other than the descendants of Adam. In (9) several narrations, they are a prolific people; no male or female among them dies until a thousand children are born to them, and they are more numerous than the rest of humanity, to the point that they were counted in (1) some narrations as nine times the number of humans. It was narrated (2) that they are of such strength and ferocity that they do not pass by a beast, a predator, or a human except that they prey upon it and eat it; nor by a crop or tree except that they graze upon it; nor by the water of a river except that they drink it and dry it up. It was narrated (3) that they are two nations, each of which is four hundred thousand nations, each nation's number being known only to Allah, the Glorified.

It was narrated (4) that they are three groups: one group like the cedar (الأرز), which is a tall tree; one group whose length and width are equal: four cubits by four cubits; and one group—who are the fiercest—each of whom has two ears: he spreads one of them and covers himself with the other. He spends the winter in one of them, wearing it, and it is hairy on its back and front; and he spends the summer in the other, and it is downy on its back and front. They are tough, with hair on their bodies that covers them. It was narrated that one (5) of them is a span, or two spans, or three. It was narrated (6) that those who used to fight them had faces like the faces of dogs.

Among them is the difference in the time of his reign: in some (7), it was after Noah. It was narrated (8) that he was in the time of Abraham and was his contemporary; he performed pilgrimage to the House, met him, and shook his hand—and it was the first handshake on the face of the earth. It was narrated (9) that he was in the time of David.

Among them is the difference in the duration of his reign: thirty years was narrated (1) and twelve years was narrated (2), among other aspects of difference that one who reviews the reports of the story in the collections of hadith—especially *al-Durr al-Manthūr*, *al-Biḥār*, *al-Burhān*, and *Nūr al-Thaqalayn*—will encounter.

In the book *Kamāl al-Dīn*, with its chain to al-Aṣbagh ibn Nubātah, who said: "Ibn al-Kawwā’ stood up to ‘Alī (peace be upon him) while he was on the pulpit and said: 'O Commander of the Faithful, inform me about *Dhū al-Qarnayn*: was he a prophet or a king? And inform me about his two horns: were they of gold or of silver?' He said to him: 'He was neither a prophet nor a king (ملك), and his two horns were neither of gold nor silver. Rather, he was a servant who loved Allah, so Allah loved him, and he was sincere toward Allah, so Allah was sincere toward him. He was only named *Dhū al-Qarnayn* because he called his people to Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, and they struck him on his horn, so he disappeared from them for a time. Then he returned to them and was struck on his other horn. And among you is one like him.'"

**I say:** It is apparent that "king" (الملك) in the narration is with a *fatḥah* on the *lām* (meaning angel), not with a *kasrah* (meaning king), due to the widespread narrations from him and others (peace be upon him) regarding the kingship of *Dhū al-Qarnayn*. Thus, his (peace be upon him) denial that *Dhū al-Qarnayn* was a prophet or an angel (ملك) is a denial of the correctness of what was reported from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in some narrations that he was a prophet, and in others that he was an angel from the angels—and this was the view of ‘Umar ibn al-Khaṭṭāb as previously indicated.

His saying: "And among you is one like him"—meaning like *Dhū al-Qarnayn* in his two wounds—he (peace be upon him) is referring to himself, for he was struck by a blow from ‘Amr ibn ‘Abd Wudd and by a blow from ‘Abd al-Raḥmān ibn Muljam (may Allah curse him), by which he was martyred. This is according to a widespread narration from him (peace be upon him) reported by both the Shī‘ah and the Sunnīs with different wordings; the most detailed of its wordings is what we have cited. The hand of "narration by meaning" (النقل بالمعنى) has played with it, making it appear in strange forms, and distortion reached its limit through it.

In *al-Durr al-Manthūr*, Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Sālim ibn Abī al-Ja‘d, who said: "‘Alī was asked about *Dhū al-Qarnayn*: 'Is he a prophet?' He said: 'I heard your Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) say: He is a servant who was sincere toward Allah, so He was sincere toward him.'"

In the *Iḥtijāj* of al-Ṭabarsī, from Imam al-Ṣādiq (peace be upon him) in a long hadith, in which the questioner said: "Inform me about the sun: where does it set?" He said: "Indeed, some of the scholars said: 'When it descends below the dome, the celestial sphere rotates with it to the belly of the sky, ascending forever until it descends to the place of its rising.'

Meaning that it sets in a muddy spring, then it pierces the earth returning to the place of its rising, so it remains bewildered under the Throne until it is permitted to rise. Its light is stripped away every day and it is clothed in a red light."

**I say:** His saying: "When it descends below the dome... to its rising" is an explanation of the sun's journey from the time of its setting until the time of its rising in its celestial orbit according to what the Ptolemaic astronomy (هيئة بطليموس)—prevalent in those ages—assumes, based on the stillness of the terrestrial globe and the movement of the celestial bodies around it. Therefore, he (peace be upon him) attributed it to "some of the scholars."

His saying: "Meaning that it sets in a muddy spring, then it pierces the earth returning to the place of its rising" is from the speech of some of the narrators of the report, not from his (peace be upon him) speech. The narrator, due to a deficiency in his understanding, interpreted His saying, the Exalted: "setting in a muddy spring" as the falling of the solar disk into the spring and its disappearance within it, then its swimming in it like a fish in water and its piercing the earth until it reaches the rising place, then its going under the Throne—which is, according to his claim, a heaven above the seven heavens or a luminous body of the greatest size placed above the seventh heaven—and its staying there until it is permitted to rise, so it is clothed in red light and rises.

The narrator is referring by his saying: "so it remains bewildered under the Throne until it is permitted etc." to another narration reported from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) that the angels take the sun after its setting and stop it under the Throne while it is stripped of light, so it stays there not knowing what it will be commanded tomorrow until it is clothed in light and commanded to rise. The narration. His understanding has committed in the interpretation of the "Throne" there something similar to what it committed in the interpretation of the "setting" here, thus increasing in distance from the truth.

The interpretation of the "Throne" has not been reported in any reliable Book or Sunnah as the ninth celestial sphere or as a luminous celestial body in the form of a bed (سرير) which his understanding invented. We have already presented most of the narrations of the Throne at the beginning of the eighth volume of this book.

In the naming—meaning his saying: "Some of the scholars said"—there is some indication that this view was not pleasing to him (peace be upon him). Despite that, he was not able to permit the truth of the matter—how could he? If the simplicity of understanding in a hypothesis easy to conceive for its people in those ages led them to this course, what do you think of them if there were cast to them that which their outward sense does not believe nor the vessel of their thought encompass?

In *al-Durr al-Manthūr*, ‘Abd al-Razzāq, Sa‘īd ibn Manṣūr, Ibn Jarīr, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Abī Ḥātim extracted through the chain of ‘Uthmān ibn Abī Ḥāḍir: "That Ibn ‘Abbās mentioned to him that Mu‘āwiyah ibn Abī Sufyān recited the verse in Surah al-Kahf: 'setting in a hot spring' (عَيْنٍ حامِيَةٍ). Ibn ‘Abbās said: 'I said to Mu‘āwiyah: We do not recite it except as *ḥami’ah* (muddy).' So Mu‘āwiyah asked ‘Abd Allāh ibn ‘Umar: 'How do you recite it?' ‘Abd Allāh said: 'As you recited it.'

Ibn ‘Abbās said: 'I said to Mu‘āwiyah: In my house the Quran was revealed.' So he sent for Ka‘b and said to him: 'Where do you find the sun setting in the Torah?' Ka‘b said to him: 'Ask the people of Arabic, for they are more knowledgeable of it. As for me, I find the sun setting in the Torah in water and mud,' and he pointed with his hand toward the West. Ibn Abī Ḥāḍir said: 'If I were with you both, I would have supported you with speech by which you would increase in insight regarding *ḥami’ah*.' Ibn ‘Abbās said: 'And what is it?' I said: 'Regarding what we report of the saying of *Tubba‘*—in what he mentioned of *Dhū al-Qarnayn* in his devotion to knowledge and his following of it:

*Dhū al-Qarnayn was a long-lived Muslim,*
*A king to whom kings submitted and gathered.*
*He reached the Easts and the Wests seeking,*
*The means of dominion from a wise guide.*
*So he saw the setting place of the sun at its setting,*
*In a spring of mud (khulub), slime (tha’ṭ), and blackness (ḥarmad).*

Ibn ‘Abbās said: 'What is *al-khulub*?' I said: 'Mud in their language.' He said: 'What is *al-tha’ṭ*?' I said: 'The black mud (الحمأة).' He said: 'What is *al-ḥarmad*?' I said: 'The black.' So Ibn ‘Abbās called a boy and said to him: 'Write down what this man says.'"

**I say:** The hadith does not harmonize well with what they have gone toward regarding the mass-transmission (تواتر) of the recitations. From the *Tījān* of Ibn Hishām is the hadith, and in it, Ibn ‘Abbās recited these verses to Mu‘āwiyah, and Mu‘āwiyah asked him about the meaning of *al-khulub*, *al-tha’ṭ*, and *al-ḥarmad*. He said: *al-khulub* is the black mud, *al-tha’ṭ* is the clay beneath it, and *al-ḥarmad* is the pebbles and stones beneath that. He cited the poem. This difference signals something regarding the narration.

In the *Tafsir* of al-‘Ayyāshī, from Abū Baṣīr from Imam al-Bāqir (peace be upon him): "Regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: «لَمْ نَجْعَلْ لَهُمْ مِنْ دُونِها سِتْراً» — *We had not provided for them any shelter against it*—he said: 'They did not know the craft of houses.'"

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummī: "Regarding the verse, he said: 'They did not know the craft of clothing.'"

In *al-Durr al-Manthūr*, Ibn al-Mundhir extracted from Ibn ‘Abbās: "Regarding His saying: «حَتَّى إِذا بَلَغَ بَيْنَ السَّدَّيْنِ» — *until when he reached [a place] between the two barriers*—he said: 'The two mountains of Armenia and Azerbaijan.'"

In the *Tafsir* of al-‘Ayyāshī, from al-Mufaḍḍal, who said: "I asked al-Ṣādiq (peace be upon him) about His saying: «أَجْعَلْ بَيْنَكُمْ وَبَيْنَهُمْ رَدْماً» — *I will set up a rampart between you and them*—he said: 'It is precautionary dissimulation (التقية) — *the practice of concealing one's faith or intentions to avoid harm*. «فَمَا اسْطاعُوا أَنْ يَظْهَرُوهُ وَمَا اسْتَطاعُوا لَهُ نَقْباً» — *So they could not scale it, nor could they dig a hole through it*—when you act with *taqiyyah*, they cannot find a way against you, and it is the fortified fortress. It becomes between you and the enemies of Allah a barrier they cannot pierce.'"

In it also, from Jābir from him (peace be upon him) regarding the verse: "He said: 'Precautionary dissimulation (التقية).'"

**I say:** The two narrations are of the type of "application" (الجري) and are not literal interpretation (تفسير).

In the *Tafsir* of al-‘Ayyāshī, from al-Aṣbagh ibn Nubātah from ‘Alī (peace be upon him): "«وَتَرَكْنا بَعْضَهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَمُوجُ فِي بَعْضٍ» — *On that day We will leave them to surge against one another*—meaning the Day of Resurrection."

**I say:** The apparent meaning of the verse according to the context is that it is from the signs of the Hour, and perhaps that is what is meant by the Day of Resurrection, for it is sometimes applied to the appearance of its precursors.

In it, from Muḥammad ibn Ḥakīm, who said: "I wrote a note to Abū ‘Abd Allāh (peace be upon him) in which was: 'Can the soul attain knowledge (المعرفة)?' He said: 'No.' I said: 'Allah says: «الَّذِينَ كانَتْ أَعْيُنُهُمْ فِي غِطاءٍ عَنْ ذِكْرِي وَكانُوا لا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ سَمْعاً» — *those whose eyes were under a veil from My remembrance, and who could not bear to hear*.' He said: 'It is like His saying: «ما كانُوا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ السَّمْعَ وَما كانُوا يُبْصِرُونَ» — *They could not bear to hear, nor did they use to see*.' I said: 'So He blamed them?' He said: 'He did not blame them for what He did to them, but He blamed them for what they did. Had they not burdened themselves, there would have been nothing against them.'"

**I say:** This means that they themselves caused this veil (الحجاب), and thus its consequence returned upon them.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, regarding the verse, he said: "They would not look at what Allah created—of the signs (الآيات), the heavens, and the earth."

**I say:** In *al-Uyun*, narrated from Imam al-Rida (peace be upon him): the application of the verse is to those who deny the Mastership (الولاية) — *the divinely ordained authority of the Ahl al-Bayt* — and this is a matter of esoteric application (الجري).

## (A Discussion Regarding the Story of Dhu al-Qarnayn)

This is a Quranic and historical inquiry divided into several chapters:

**1 - The Story of Dhu al-Qarnayn in the Quran:** The Quran did not address his name, the date of his birth or life, his lineage, or other identifying characteristics, as is its custom when mentioning the stories of the ancients. Rather, it sufficed with mentioning three of his journeys. The first journey was to the West, until when he reached the setting-place of the sun, he found it setting in a muddy (or hot) spring and found a people near it. The second journey was to the East, until when he reached the rising-place of the sun, he found it rising upon a people for whom Allah had provided no shelter against it. The third journey was until when he reached between the two barriers, he found besides them a people who could scarcely understand any speech. They complained to him about the corruption of Gog (يأجوج) and Magog (مأجوج) in the land and offered to pay him a tribute on the condition that he place a barrier between them and Gog and Magog. He agreed to build the barrier and promised to build for them something beyond their expectations. He refused to accept their tribute, requesting only that they assist him with strength; the story mentions men, blocks of iron, bellows, and molten copper (القطر).

The essential characteristics and aspects derived from the story are: First, the subject of the story was called "Dhu al-Qarnayn" (the Two-Horned One) even before the revelation of his story in the Quran, and even during his lifetime, as appears from the context of the story in His sayings: **يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنْ ذِي الْقَرْنَيْنِ** "They ask you about Dhul Qarnayn" (18:83), **قُلْنا يا ذَا الْقَرْنَيْنِ** "We said, ‘O Dhul Qarnayn!’" (18:86), and **قالُوا يا ذَا الْقَرْنَيْنِ** "They said, ‘O Dhul Qarnayn!’" (18:94).

Second: He was a believer in Allah and the Last Day, and a follower of the true religion, as appears from his saying: **هذا رَحْمَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّي فَإِذا جاءَ وَعْدُ رَبِّي جَعَلَهُ دَكَّاءَ وَكانَ وَعْدُ رَبِّي حَقًّا** "‘This is a mercy from my Lord. But when the promise of my Lord comes to pass, He will level it, and the promise of my Lord is true.’" (18:98) and his saying: **أَمَّا مَنْ ظَلَمَ فَسَوْفَ نُعَذِّبُهُ ثُمَّ يُرَدُّ إِلى رَبِّهِ فَيُعَذِّبُهُ عَذاباً نُكْراً وَأَمَّا مَنْ آمَنَ وَعَمِلَ صالِحاً** "‘As for him who is a wrongdoer, we will punish him, then he will be returned to his Lord and He will punish him with a terrible punishment. But as for him who has faith and acts righteously...’" (18:87-88). What increases his religious nobility is that the saying of the Exalted: **قُلْنا يا ذَا الْقَرْنَيْنِ إِمَّا أَنْ تُعَذِّبَ وَإِمَّا أَنْ تَتَّخِذَ فِيهِمْ حُسْناً** "We said, ‘O Dhul Qarnayn! Either punish them, or treat them with kindness.’" (18:86) indicates that he was supported by revelation (الوحي), inspiration (الإلهام), or a prophet from the prophets of Allah who was with him, guiding him by conveying the revelation.

Third: He was among those for whom Allah gathered the good of this world and the Hereafter. As for the good of this world, it was the great kingdom through which he reached the setting and rising places of the sun; nothing stood in his way, and all means (الأسباب) were made subservient to him. As for the good of the Hereafter, it was the spreading of justice, the establishment of truth, forgiveness, pardon, gentleness, nobility of soul, the dissemination of good, and the repelling of evil. All of this is indicated by the saying of the Exalted: **إِنَّا مَكَّنَّا لَهُ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَآتَيْناهُ مِنْ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ سَبَباً** "Indeed We granted him power in the land and gave him the means to all things." (18:84), in addition to what is understood from the context of the story regarding his physical and spiritual dominance.

Fourth: He encountered a wrongdoing people in the West and punished them.

Fifth: The rampart (الردم) he built is located neither at the setting-place of the sun nor its rising-place; for after reaching the rising-place of the sun, he followed a course until he reached between the two barriers. Among the characteristics of his rampart—besides being located somewhere other than the West or East—is that it is situated between two mountains like two walls, that he leveled the gap between their two cliffs (الصدفين), and that he used blocks of iron and molten copper in its construction. Inevitably, it is in a mountain pass (مضيق) that connects two regions of the inhabited earth.

**2 - Mention of Dhu al-Qarnayn, the Barrier, and Gog and Magog:** In the reports of the ancients, the early historians did not mention in their accounts a king called "Dhu al-Qarnayn" during his era, nor any term carrying that meaning in a non-Arabic language. Nor did they mention "Gog and Magog" by these two specific terms, nor a barrier attributed to him by his name. However, some poems of pride are attributed to certain Himyarite kings of Yemen, in which Dhu al-Qarnayn is mentioned as one of their ancestors, the *Tababi'ah* (التبابعة). These poems mention his journey to the West and East and the barrier of Gog and Magog; a portion of these will be presented to you in the coming chapters.

The mention of "Magog" and "Gog and Magog" appears in several places in the books of the Old Testament. In the tenth chapter (الإصحاح) of the Book of Genesis in the Torah: "This is the account of Shem, Ham and Japheth, Noah’s sons, who themselves had sons after the flood. The sons of Japheth: Gomer, Magog, Madai, Javan, Tubal, Meshech and Tiras."

In the Book of Ezekiel, chapter thirty-eight: "The word of the Lord came to me: 'Son of man, set your face against Gog, of the land of Magog, the chief prince of Meshech and Tubal; prophesy against him and say: "This is what the Sovereign Lord says: I am against you, Gog, chief prince of Meshech and Tubal. I will turn you around, put hooks in your jaws and bring you out with your whole army—your horses, your horsemen fully armed, and a great horde with large and small shields, all of them brandishing their swords. Persia, Cush and Put will be with them, all with shields and helmets, also Gomer with all its troops, and Beth Togarmah from the far north with all its troops—the many nations with you."'"

It says: "'Therefore, son of man, prophesy and say to Gog: "This is what the Sovereign Lord says: In that day, when my people Israel are living in safety, will you not take notice of it? You will come from your place in the far north..."'" etc.

And it says in chapter thirty-nine, continuing the previous discourse: "'Son of man, prophesy against Gog and say: "This is what the Sovereign Lord says: I am against you, Gog, chief prince of Meshech and Tubal. I will turn you around and drag you along. I will bring you from the far north and send you against the mountains of Israel. Then I will strike your bow from your left hand and make your arrows drop from your right hand. On the mountains of Israel you will fall, you and all your troops and the nations with you. I will give you as food to all kinds of carrion birds and to the wild animals. You will fall in the open field, for I have spoken, declares the Sovereign Lord. I will send fire on Magog and on those who live in safety in the coastlands, and they will know that I am the Lord."'" etc.

In the Revelation of John, chapter twenty: "And I saw an angel coming down out of heaven, having the key to the Abyss and holding in his hand a great chain. He seized the dragon, that ancient serpent, who is the devil, or Satan, and bound him for a thousand years. He threw him into the Abyss, and locked and sealed it over him, to keep him from leading the nations astray anymore until the thousand years were ended. After that, he must be set free for a short time."

It says: "When the thousand years are over, Satan will be released from his prison and will go out to deceive the nations in the four corners of the earth—Gog and Magog—and to gather them for battle. In number they are like the sand on the seashore. They marched across the breadth of the earth and surrounded the camp of God’s people, the city he loves. But fire came down from heaven and devoured them. And the devil, who deceived them, was thrown into the lake of burning sulfur, where the beast and the false prophet had been thrown. They will be tormented day and night for ever and ever."

It is understood from this that "Magog" or "Gog and Magog" were a great nation or nations inhabiting the far north of Asia in the inhabited world of that time, and that they were warlike nations known for conquests and raids.

It is plausible then to surmise that this Dhu al-Qarnayn was one of the great kings who blocked the path of these corrupting nations. The barrier attributed to him must be a separator between the northern region of the Asian continent and its south, such as the Great Wall of China, the Barrier of *Bab al-Abwab*, the "Darial" Barrier, or others.

Modern histories of nations have accepted that the northeastern region of Asia—the highlands and plateaus in northern China—was the homeland of a large, nomadic, and barbaric nation that continuously increased in number and density. They would raid neighboring nations like China, or sometimes descend from their highlands into Central and Lower Asia, even reaching Northern Europe. Some settled in the lands they raided—as did most inhabitants of Northern Europe—becoming civilized and engaging in agriculture and industry, while others returned and then came back again and again.

This also supports what we previously deemed probable: that the barrier we are searching for is one of the existing barriers in Northern Asia that separates the north from the south.

## 3 - Who is Dhu al-Qarnayn? And where is his barrier?

Historians and exegetes have various opinions on this, depending on their different views in applying the story:

**A -** It is attributed to some that the barrier mentioned in the Quran is the Great Wall of China. This is a long wall that separates China from Mongolia, built by "Qin Shi Huang," one of the kings of China, to repel Mongol attacks. Its length is three thousand kilometers, its width nine meters, and its height fifteen meters, built with stones. Its construction began in 264 BC and was completed in ten or twenty years. According to this, Dhu al-Qarnayn is the aforementioned king.

This is refuted by the fact that the descriptions and characteristics mentioned in the Quran for Dhu al-Qarnayn and his barrier do not apply to this king or the Great Wall of China. It is not recorded that this king traveled from his land to the farthest West. Furthermore, the barrier mentioned in the Quran is described as a rampart (ردم) between two mountains, using blocks of iron and molten copper (القطر). The Great Wall extends for three thousand kilometers, passing through plains and mountains; it is not between two mountains, and it is built of stone, with no iron or copper used in it.

**B -** It is attributed to some that the one who built the barrier was one of the kings of Assyria. Around the seventh century BC, Scythian (سيت) tribes attacked from the Caucasus mountain passes into Armenia, then western Iran, and perhaps reached the land of Assyria and its capital, Nineveh, surrounding them with killing, captivity, and looting. Thus, the King of Assyria built the barrier to repel them. It is as if the intended barrier is the Barrier of *Bab al-Abwab*, whose construction or restoration is attributed to Khosrow Anushirvan of the Persian kings. However, the issue remains in matching the specific details of the story to him.

**C -** It is said in *Ruh al-Ma'ani*: It is said that he—meaning Dhu al-Qarnayn—is Fereydun ibn Athfiyan ibn Jamshid, the fifth of the Pishdadian kings of Persia. He was a just king, obedient to Allah the Exalted. In the book *Suwar al-Aqalim* by Abu Zayd al-Balkhi, it is stated that he was supported by revelation. In general histories, it is mentioned that he ruled the earth and divided it among his three sons: Iraj, Salm, and Tur. He gave Iraj Iraq, India, and the Hijaz, making him the master of the crown; he gave Salm Rome, Egypt, and the West; and he gave Tur China, the Turks, and the East. He established a law for each to rule by, and these three laws were called "Siyasa," which is the Arabized form of "Si Ayasa," meaning "three laws."

The reason for his being named "Dhu al-Qarnayn" (the Two-Horned) is that he ruled the two ends of the world, or because of the length of his reign—which, according to *Rawdat al-Safa*, was five hundred years—or due to his great courage and his subjugation of kings. (End of quote).

The critique of this is that history does not recognize this account.

**D -** It is said that Dhu al-Qarnayn is Alexander the Great (the Macedonian). This is the most famous view among the people, and "Alexander's Barrier" is a common proverb among them. This has appeared in some narrations, such as the narration in *Qurb al-Isnad* from Imam Musa ibn Ja'far (peace be upon him), and the narration of Uqbah ibn Amir from the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), and the narration of Wahb ibn Munabbih, both recorded in *al-Durr al-Manthur*.

This was the view of some early exegetes among the Companions and Successors, such as Mu'adh ibn Jabal—as mentioned in *Majma' al-Bayan*—and Qatadah, as narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur*. Shaykh Abu Ali ibn Sina (Avicenna), when mentioning him in the book *al-Shifa*, described him as Dhu al-Qarnayn, and Imam al-Razi insisted on this in his *Tafsir al-Kabir*.

Al-Razi said, in summary: The Quran indicates that the man's kingdom reached the farthest West, the farthest East, and the North. This constitutes the entirety of the inhabited earth. Such a kingdom must have its name immortalized. The king famous in history books whose kingdom reached this extent is none other than Alexander.

This is because after his father's death, he gathered the kings of Rome and the West, conquered them, and reached the Green Sea, then Egypt, where he built Alexandria. Then he entered the Levant, sought the Children of Israel, arrived at Jerusalem, and sacrificed at its altar. Then he turned toward Armenia and *Bab al-Abwab*. The Iraqis, Copts, and Berbers submitted to him. He seized Iran, sought India and China, campaigned against distant nations, returned to Khorasan, built many cities, then returned to Iraq and died in Shahrizor or Rumiyyat al-Mada'in. He was carried to Alexandria and buried there. He lived thirty-three years, and the duration of his reign was twelve years.

Since it is established by the Quran that Dhu al-Qarnayn ruled most of the inhabited world, and established by history that the one who fits this description is Alexander, it must be concluded that Dhu al-Qarnayn is Alexander. (End of quote).

The critique of this is: First, his claim that the rule of most of the inhabited world was exclusive to Alexander the Great is not historically accepted; there are kings who equaled or exceeded him in dominion.

Second: The attributes of Dhu al-Qarnayn mentioned in the Quran are either not historically accepted for Alexander or are denied of him. The Quran mentions that Dhu al-Qarnayn was a believer in Allah and the Last Day—which is the religion of monotheism (التوحيد)—whereas Alexander was a pagan Sabian, as it is recounted that he offered a sacrifice to Jupiter. The Quran mentions that Dhu al-Qarnayn was among the righteous servants of Allah, possessing justice and gentleness, while history recounts the opposite for Alexander.

Third: It is not mentioned in any of their histories that Alexander the Great built the barrier of Gog and Magog as described in the Quran.

It is said in *al-Bidayah wa al-Nihayah* regarding the report of Dhu al-Qarnayn: Ishaq ibn Bishr said from Sa'id ibn Bashir from Qatadah: "Alexander is Dhu al-Qarnayn, and his father was the first of the Caesars, and he was from the descendants of Shem ibn Noah. As for the second Dhu al-Qarnayn, he is Alexander ibn Philip (he then traced his lineage to Esau ibn Isaac ibn Abraham), the Macedonian, Greek, Egyptian, builder of Alexandria, by whose days the Romans date their history. He was much later than the first one."

This was about three hundred years before Christ. Aristotle the philosopher was his vizier. He is the one who killed Darius ibn Darius, humiliated the Persian kings, and trampled their land.

He said: "We have pointed this out because many people believe they are one and the same, and that the one mentioned in the Quran is the one whose vizier was Aristotle. Because of this, a great error, widespread corruption, and much confusion occur. For the first was a righteous believing servant and a just king, and his vizier was al-Khidr—who was a prophet according to what we established before. As for the second, he was a polytheist, and his vizier was a philosopher. There were more than two thousand years between their times. Where is this one from that one? They are neither equal nor similar, except to an ignorant person who does not know the realities of matters." (End of quote).

This contains a critique of Imam al-Razi's previous statement. However, if you examine what we quoted from his words and then refer to his book regarding the story of Dhu al-Qarnayn, you will find that he commits no less an error than that committed by Imam al-Razi. For there is no trace in history of a king who lived more than two thousand three hundred years before Christ, ruled the earth from the farthest West to the farthest East and the North, built the barrier, was a righteous believer—even a prophet—whose vizier was al-Khidr, and entered the darkness in search of the Water of Life, whether his name was Alexander or otherwise.

**E -** A group of historians mentioned that Dhu al-Qarnayn was one of the *Tababi'ah* (التبابعة)—the "Dhus" (الأذواء) of Yemen among the Himyarite kings of Yemen. This is the view of al-Asma'i in his history of the Arabs before Islam, Ibn Hisham in the *Sirah*, *al-Tijan*, Abu Rayhan al-Biruni in *al-Athar al-Baqiyah*, and Nashwan ibn Sa'id al-Himyari in *Shams al-Ulum*—according to what is narrated from them—and others.

They differed regarding his name. It was said his name was Mus'ab ibn Abdullah; it was said Sa'b ibn Dhi al-Mara'id, who was the first of the *Tababi'ah* and the one who judged for Abraham at Beersheba; it was said Tubba' al-Aqran and his name was Hassan. Al-Asma'i mentioned that he was As'ad al-Kamil, the fourth of the *Tababi'ah*, son of Hassan al-Aqran, son of Malki Karib Tubba' II, son of King Tubba' I. It was also said his name was "Shammar Yar'ish."

The mention of Dhu al-Qarnayn and pride in him has appeared in several poems of the Himyarites and some pre-Islamic poets. In *al-Bidayah wa al-Nihayah*, Ibn Hisham quoted al-A'sha:

*And al-Sa'b, Dhu al-Qarnayn, has become a dweller*
*In the hollow of a lofty, permanent grave.*

It was mentioned in the previous traditional inquiry that Uthman ibn Abi al-Hadir recited to Ibn Abbas the words of a Himyarite:

*Dhu al-Qarnayn was my grandfather, a Muslim*
*A king to whom kings submitted and gathered.*
*He reached the Easts and Wests seeking*
*The means of an affair from a Wise Guide.*
*So he saw the setting-place of the sun at its setting*
*In a spring of mud and dark, black slime.*

Al-Maqrizi said in *al-Khitat*: "Know that the verification according to the scholars of reports is that the Dhu al-Qarnayn whom Allah mentioned in His Mighty Book, saying: **وَيَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنْ ذِي الْقَرْنَيْنِ قُلْ سَأَتْلُوا عَلَيْكُمْ مِنْهُ ذِكْراً إِنَّا مَكَّنَّا لَهُ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَآتَيْناهُ مِنْ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ سَبَباً** 'They ask you about Dhul Qarnayn. Say, "I will relate to you a narrative of him." Indeed We granted him power in the land and gave him the means to all things' (18:83-84), is an Arab who is frequently mentioned in the poetry of the Arabs."

"And that his name is al-Sa'b ibn Dhi Mara'id ibn al-Harith al-Ra'ish ibn al-Hamal Dhi Sadad ibn Ad Dhi Manah ibn Ar al-Maltat ibn Saksak ibn Wa'il ibn Himyar ibn Saba' ibn Yashjub ibn Ya'rub ibn Qahtan ibn Hud ibn Abir ibn Shalikh ibn Arpachshad ibn Shem ibn Noah (peace be upon him)."

"And that he was a king from the kings of Himyar, and they are the pure Arabs (العرب العاربة). They are also called the 'Arabized Arabs' (العرب العرباء). Dhu al-Qarnayn was a crowned *Tubba'*. When he assumed kingship, he was haughty, then he humbled himself before Allah and met with al-Khidr. He who thinks that Alexander ibn Philip is the Dhu al-Qarnayn who built the barrier is mistaken, for the word 'Dhu' is Arabic, and 'Dhu al-Qarnayn' is among the titles of the Arab kings of Yemen, whereas that one is a Roman Greek."

Abu Ja'far al-Tabari said: "Al-Khidr was in the days of Fereydun the King, son of al-Dahhak, according to the view of the generality of the scholars of the early People of the Book. It was also said he was in the time of Moses ibn Imran (peace be upon him). It was also said that he was at the vanguard of the elder Dhu al-Qarnayn who was in the days of Abraham the Friend (peace be upon him), and that al-Khidr reached the River of Life during Dhu al-Qarnayn's journey through the lands and drank from its water—without Dhu al-Qarnayn or those with him knowing—so he became immortal and is alive according to them until now. Others said that the Dhu al-Qarnayn who was in the time of Abraham the Friend (peace be upon him) was Fereydun ibn al-Dahhak, and al-Khidr was at his vanguard."

Abu Muhammad Abd al-Malik ibn Hisham said in the book *al-Tijan* regarding the knowledge of the kings of the time, after mentioning the lineage of Dhu al-Qarnayn that we cited: "He was a crowned *Tubba'*. When he assumed kingship, he was haughty, then he humbled himself and met al-Khidr in Jerusalem.
