He traveled with him to the Easts and Wests of the earth and was given the means to all things as Allah the Exalted informed. He built the barrier against Gog and Magog and died in Iraq."

As for Alexander, he is Greek, known as Alexander the Macedonian. Ibn Abbas was asked about Dhu al-Qarnayn: "From whom was he?" He replied: "From Himyar, and he is al-Sa'b ibn Dhi Mara'id, whom Allah empowered in the land and gave the means to all things. He reached the two horns of the sun and the head of the earth, and built the barrier against Gog and Magog." It was said to him: "And Alexander?" He said: "He was a righteous Roman man, a sage, who built a lighthouse on the sea in Africa, took the land of Rome, came to the Arabian Sea, and performed many works among the Arabs of structures and cities."

Ka'b al-Ahbar was asked about Dhu al-Qarnayn, and he said: "The correct view according to our rabbis and ancestors is that he was from Himyar, and that he was al-Sa'b ibn Dhi Mara'id. Alexander was a man from Greece, from the descendants of Esau ibn Isaac ibn Abraham the Friend (peace be upon him). The men of Alexander reached the time of the Messiah, son of Mary—among them were Galen and Aristotle."

Al-Hamdani said in the *Book of Lineages*: "Kahlan ibn Saba' begat Zayd, and Zayd begat Arib, Malik, Ghalib, and Amikarab." Al-Haytham said: "Amikarab ibn Saba' was the brother of Himyar and Kahlan. Amikarab begat Abu Malik, Fadrah, and Muhaylil. Ghalib begat Junadah ibn Ghalib, who ruled after Muhaylil ibn Amikarab ibn Saba'. Arib begat Amr, and Amr begat Zayd and al-Humays'—whose kunya was Abu al-Sa'b, and he is the first Dhu al-Qarnayn, the surveyor and builder. Regarding him, al-Nu'man ibn Bashir said:

*Who among the people would oppose us, a noble folk?*
*For Dhu al-Qarnayn is from us, and Hatim.*

Regarding him, al-Harithi said:

*Name for us one of you whom we might know*
*In the Pre-Islamic era, bearing the name of a king,*
*Like the two Tubba's and Dhu al-Qarnayn; accepted*
*By the people of intellect, for the truest speech is what is accepted.*

Regarding him, Ibn Abi Dhi'b al-Khuza'i said:

*And from us is he who traveled to the two horizons,*
*Ascending in every land and descending.*
*He reached the horn of the sun, East and West,*
*And in the rampart of Gog, he built and then established.*
*That is Dhu al-Qarnayn; Himyar takes pride*
*In an army of elephants that cannot be counted or reckoned.*

Al-Hamdani said: "The scholars of Hamdan say: Dhu al-Qarnayn is al-Sa'b ibn Malik ibn al-Harith al-A'la ibn Rabi'ah ibn al-Hayyar ibn Malik. There are many sayings regarding Dhu al-Qarnayn." (End of al-Maqrizi's words).

This is a comprehensive discourse, and from it, several points are derived: First, the title "Dhu al-Qarnayn" was used by more than one of the Himyarite kings, and there was a "First (Great) Dhu al-Qarnayn" and others.

Second: The first Dhu al-Qarnayn—the one who built the barrier of Gog and Magog—lived many centuries before Alexander the Great, whether he was a contemporary of the Friend (peace be upon him) or after him, as necessitated by Ibn Hisham's report of his meeting al-Khidr in Jerusalem, which was built several centuries after Abraham in the time of David and Solomon (peace be upon them). In any case, he was before him, despite the ambiguity in the history of the Himyarite kings.

The discussion remains regarding what he mentioned and chose from two perspectives:

One: Where is the location of this barrier built by the Himyarite *Tubba'*?

Two: Who are these corrupting nations in the land for whose repulsion the barrier was built? Is this barrier one of the barriers built in Yemen or its vicinity, like the Marib Dam and others? Those were barriers built to store water for drinking and irrigation, not for repulsion, and they did not involve the use of iron blocks and molten copper as Allah mentioned in His Book. Or was it another? And was there a corrupting, attacking nation there, when their neighbors were only the likes of the Copts, Assyrians, Chaldeans, and others, who were people of civilization and urbanity?

One of the eminent contemporary researchers, in support of this view, stated in summary: The Dhu al-Qarnayn mentioned in the Quran lived hundreds of years before Alexander the Great, so he is not him. Rather, he is one of the righteous kings from the *Tababi'ah* "Dhus" of the kings of Yemen. It was the custom of a group of them to be named with "Dhu," such as Dhu Hamdan, Dhu Ghamdan, Dhu al-Manar, Dhu al-Adh'ar, Dhu Yazan, and others.

He was a Muslim monotheist, just, of good conduct, powerful, and awe-inspiring. He traveled with a massive army toward the West, seizing Egypt and what lay beyond it. He continued traveling along the coasts of the Mediterranean until he reached the coast of the Western Ocean and found the sun setting in a muddy or hot spring.

Then he returned, traveling toward the East, and built "Africa" during his journey. He was intensely fond of and experienced in building and construction. He continued traveling until he passed through the peninsula and the wildernesses of Central Asia, reaching Turkestan and the Wall of China, where he found a people for whom Allah had provided no shelter from the sun.

Then he inclined toward the northern side until he reached the Tropic of Cancer. Perhaps it is he of whom it is commonly said that he entered the darkness. The people of those lands asked him to build a barrier for them to repel Gog and Magog, as the Yemenis—and Dhu al-Qarnayn was among them—were known for building barriers and having expertise in them. So he built the barrier for them.

If this barrier is the Great Wall separating China from Mongolia, then it was a restoration by him of parts that had collapsed over time; otherwise, the original wall was built by some Chinese kings before that. If it was another barrier besides the Great Wall, there is no issue. Among what Dhu al-Qarnayn—whose original name was "Shammar Yar'ish"—built in those regions was the city of Samarkand, according to what is said.

He supported this by saying that Dhu al-Qarnayn being a righteous Arab king about whom the Bedouins asked the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his family), and whom the Quran mentions for remembrance and reflection, is more acceptable than mentioning the story of some Roman, Persian, or Chinese kings. They were from distant nations for whom the Bedouins had no desire to hear their news or reflect upon their traces. Therefore, the Quran did not address any of their reports. (End of summary).

What remains problematic is that the Great Wall of China cannot be the barrier of Dhu al-Qarnayn. For Dhu al-Qarnayn lived several centuries before Alexander, as he mentioned, while the Great Wall of China was built nearly half a century after Alexander. As for barriers other than the Great Wall, there are some other barriers in the northwestern region of China, but they are built of stone, as mentioned, with no trace of iron blocks or molten copper.

It is said in *Tafsir al-Jawahir*, after mentioning an introduction summarized as: What is known of the Yemeni states with the help of inscriptions discovered in the ruins of Yemen are three states:

The State of Ma'in, its capital being Qarnaw. Scholars have estimated that the traces of the Ma'in state begin from the 14th century BC to the 7th or 8th century BC. They have found some kings of this state, twenty-six in number, such as "Ab-Yada'" and "Ab-Yada' Yathi'," meaning the Savior.

The State of Saba'. They were from the Qahtanites. They were first "Dhus" (أذواء) then "Qayls" (أقيال). The one among them who rose to prominence was "Saba'," the master of the Sarwah palace east of Sana'a, who seized control of all. Their kingdom began from 850 BC to 115 BC. Known among their kings are twenty-seven, fifteen of whom were called *Mukarrib*, such as the *Mukarrib* "Yatha' Amar" and the *Mukarrib* "Dhamar Ali," and twelve of whom were called only "King," such as King "Dharah" and King "Yarim Ayman."

The State of the Himyarites. They consist of two layers: the first being the kings of Saba' and Raydan from 115 BC to 275 AD, and these were kings only. The second layer being the kings of Saba', Raydan, Hadramaut, and others; these were fourteen kings, most of whom were *Tababi'ah*. The first was "Shammar Yar'ish," the second "Dhu al-Qarnayn," and the third "Amr," the husband of Bilqis—and it ends with Dhu Jadan. Their kingdom began from 275 AD to 525 AD.

He then said: Thus, the connection of the title "Dhu" with the kings of Yemen has become apparent. We do not find among others, such as the Roman kings for example, anyone titled with "Dhu." So Dhu al-Qarnayn is from the kings of Yemen, and there has preceded among their kings one named Dhu al-Qarnayn. But is this the Dhu al-Qarnayn mentioned in the Quran?

We say: No. Because this one is mentioned among kings very close to our time, and that was not narrated about them, except perhaps in narrations mentioned by storytellers in history, such as that "Shammar Yar'ish" reached the lands of Iraq, Persia, Khorasan, and Sogdia, and built the city of Samarkand—its origin being "Shammar-kand"—and that As'ad Abu Karib invaded Azerbaijan, sent his son Hassan to Sogdia, his son Ya'fur to Rome, and his nephew to Persia, and that some Himyarites remained in China to this day after that king's invasion of it.

Ibn Khaldun and others dismissed these reports as lies, labeling them as exaggerated, and refuted them with geographical and historical evidence.

Therefore, Dhu al-Qarnayn is from the Arab nation, but in an ancient history before the known history. (End of summary).

**And it is said:** Dhu al-Qarnayn is Cyrus (كورش), one of the Achaemenid Persian kings (539–560 BC). He is the one who founded the Iranian Empire, united the kingdoms of Persia and Media, conquered Babylon, permitted the return of the Jews from Babylon to Jerusalem, assisted in building the Temple, conquered Egypt, then crossed into Greece and defeated them, reaching the West. Then he traveled to the farthest inhabited regions in the East.

This was mentioned by someone near our era, and then some Indian researchers in recent times exerted effort in clarifying and making it plausible. The summary of this is that the description of Dhu al-Qarnayn mentioned in the Quran applies to this great king. He was a believer in Allah according to the religion of monotheism (التوحيد), just toward his subjects, traveling with compassion, gentleness, and benevolence, and firm with the people of oppression and aggression. Allah gave him the means to all things, so he combined religion, intellect, moral virtues, preparation, strength, wealth, and power, with all means being subservient to him.

He traveled, as Allah mentioned in His Book, once toward the West until he seized Lydia and its surroundings. Then he traveled a second time toward the East until he reached the rising-place of the sun and found there a nomadic, barbaric people living in the wilderness. Then he built the barrier, which, according to evidence, is the barrier constructed in the Darial Pass between the Caucasus Mountains near the city of Tbilisi. This is the summary of the discourse, and here is the detail:

**His faith in Allah and the Last Day:** This is indicated by what is in the books of the Old Testament, such as the Book of Ezra (chapter 1), the Book of Daniel (chapter 6), and the Book of Isaiah (chapters 44 and 45), regarding his glorification and sanctification, even calling him in the Book of Isaiah "the Lord's shepherd." It says in the forty-fifth chapter: "This is what the Lord says to his anointed, to Cyrus, whose right hand I take hold of to subdue nations before him and to strip kings of their armor, to open doors before him so that gates will not be shut: I will go before you and will level the mountains; I will break down gates of bronze and cut through bars of iron. I will give you hidden treasures, riches stored in secret places, so that you may know that I am the Lord, the God of Israel, who summons you by name... I bestow on you a title of honor, though you do not acknowledge me."

Even if one disregards its being revelation, the Jews, despite their religious fanaticism, would not consider a polytheistic, Magian, or pagan man—if Cyrus were such—as a divine messiah, guided, supported, and a shepherd for the Lord.

Furthermore, the inscriptions and writings in cuneiform script inherited from Darius the Great—with only eight years separating them—speak of him being a monotheist, not a polytheist. It is not conceivable that what Cyrus was upon would change in this short time.

As for his psychological virtues, it suffices to refer to the preserved reports of his life and conduct, and how he dealt with the tyrants and despots who rebelled against him or whom he fought, such as the kings of Media, Lydia, Babylon, Egypt, and the nomadic tyrants on the fringes of Bactria (which is Balkh) and others. Whenever he prevailed over a people, he pardoned their criminals, honored their noble ones, had mercy on their weak, and disciplined their corrupt and treacherous.

The books of the Old Testament praised him, and the Jews respect him with the greatest respect because he saved them from the Babylonian captivity, returned them to their lands, spent wealth for them to renew the building of the Temple, and returned to them the precious items of the Temple that had been looted and stored in the treasuries of the kings of Babylon. This in itself is another support for Dhu al-Qarnayn being Cyrus, for the question about Dhu al-Qarnayn was prompted by the Jews, according to the narrations.

The ancient Greek historians, such as Herodotus and others, could not help but describe him with chivalry, gallantry, magnanimity, generosity, forgiveness, lack of greed, mercy, and compassion, praising him with the finest praise.

**As for his being named Dhu al-Qarnayn:** Although histories are devoid of anything indicating this, the recent discovery of his stone statue in Mashhad Murghab in southern Iran removes doubt regarding his being described as "Dhu al-Qarnayn." For he is depicted there with two horns growing from the top of his head from a single base; one horn inclines forward and the other takes the backward direction. This is close to the saying of the ancients regarding the reason for the name Dhu al-Qarnayn: that he had a crown or helmet with two horns.

In the Book of Daniel, there is mention of a vision he saw regarding Cyrus in the form of a two-horned ram. It says:

"In the third year of King Belshazzar’s reign, I, Daniel, had a vision, after the one that had already appeared to me. In my vision I saw myself in the citadel of Susa in the province of Elam; in the vision I was beside the Ulai Canal. I looked up, and there before me was a ram with two horns, standing beside the canal, and the horns were long. One of the horns was longer than the other but grew up later. I watched the ram as it charged toward the west and the north and the south. No animal could stand against it, and none could rescue from its power. It did as it pleased and became great."

"As I was thinking about this, suddenly a goat with a prominent horn between its eyes came from the west, crossing the whole earth without touching the ground. It came toward the two-horned ram I had seen standing beside the canal and charged at it in great rage. I saw it attack the ram furiously, striking the ram and shattering its two horns. The ram was powerless to stand against it; the goat knocked it to the ground and trampled on it, and none could rescue the ram from its power. The goat became very great..."

Then it mentions after the completion of the vision that Gabriel appeared to him and interpreted his vision, in which the two-horned ram applies to Cyrus, and its two horns are the kingdoms of Persia and Media, and the goat with one horn applies to Alexander the Great.

As for his journey toward the West and East: his journey toward the West was to repel the tyrant of Lydia, who had marched with his armies toward Cyrus in oppression and transgression without any excuse. So he marched toward him, fought him, and defeated him, then pursued him until he besieged him in his capital, then conquered it and captured him. Then he pardoned him and all his supporters, honored them, and treated them well, though he could have disciplined or destroyed them. The application of the story to the saying of the Exalted: **حَتَّى إِذا بَلَغَ مَغْرِبَ الشَّمْسِ وَجَدَها تَغْرُبُ فِي عَيْنٍ حَمِئَةٍ** "until when he reached the setting-place of the sun, he found it setting in a muddy spring" (18:86)—perhaps the western coast of Asia Minor—**وَوَجَدَ عِنْدَها قَوْماً قُلْنا يا ذَا الْقَرْنَيْنِ إِمَّا أَنْ تُعَذِّبَ وَإِمَّا أَنْ تَتَّخِذَ فِيهِمْ حُسْناً** "and he found a people near it. We said, ‘O Dhul Qarnayn! Either punish them, or treat them with kindness’" (18:86), is due to their deserving punishment for their transgression, oppression, and corruption.

Then he traveled toward the great desert in the East around Bactria to extinguish the fire of sedition ignited by nomadic, barbaric tribes who had risen there for attack and corruption. The application of the saying of the Exalted: **حَتَّى إِذا بَلَغَ مَطْلِعَ الشَّمْسِ وَجَدَها تَطْلُعُ عَلى قَوْمٍ لَمْ نَجْعَلْ لَهُمْ مِنْ دُونِها سِتْراً** "until when he reached the rising-place of the sun, he found it rising upon a people for whom We had provided no shelter from it" (18:90), to him is apparent.

**As for his building the barrier:** The barrier exists in the pass of the Caucasus Mountains extending from the Caspian Sea to the Black Sea. The pass is called "Darial," and it is located between the city of Tbilisi and Vladikavkaz.

This barrier is located in a pass between two towering mountains that extend from its sides. It is the only opening connecting the southern and northern sides of the mountains. The mountains, along with the Caspian Sea and the Black Sea, form a natural barrier thousands of kilometers long, separating southern Asia from its north.

In those ages, evil nations from the inhabitants of northeastern Asia would attack from the Caucasus mountain pass to the regions south of it, raiding Armenia, then Iran, even Assyria and Chaldea. They attacked around the seventh century BC, spreading killing, captivity, and looting throughout the lands until they reached Nineveh, the capital of Assyria. This was approximately in the century preceding the era of Cyrus.

Ancient historians like Herodotus the Greek mentioned Cyrus's journey to northern Iran to extinguish the fires of sedition that had ignited there. It appears that he built the barrier in the Darial Pass during this journey at the request of the people of the north and their complaint. He built it with stones and iron, and it is the only rampart in which iron was used, and it is between two mountain barriers. The application of the saying of the Exalted: **فَأَعِينُونِي بِقُوَّةٍ أَجْعَلْ بَيْنَكُمْ وَبَيْنَهُمْ رَدْماً آتُونِي زُبَرَ الْحَدِيدِ** "‘So assist me with strength, and I will make a barrier between you and them. Bring me blocks of iron...’" (18:95-96) to it is apparent.

Among what supports this claim is the existence of a river near it called "Cyrus," which is the name of Cyrus among Westerners, and another river passing through Tbilisi called "Kur." This barrier was mentioned by Josephus, the Jewish historian, when mentioning his journey to northern Caucasus. It is not the wall existing in *Bab al-Abwab* on the coast of the Caspian Sea, for history attributes its construction to Khosrow Anushirvan, and Josephus was before him.

Furthermore, the barrier of *Bab al-Abwab* is certainly not the barrier of Dhu al-Qarnayn mentioned in the Quran, as iron was never used in it.

**As for Gog and Magog:** Research into the developments governing languages guides us to the fact that they are the Mongols. For the word in Chinese pronunciation is "Mankuk" or "Manjuk." The terms "Yajuj" and "Majuj" seem to be Hebrew transliterations, and in the Greek translations and others of the Old Testament, they are "Gog" and "Magog." The complete similarity between "Magog" and "Mankuk" dictates that the word developed from the Chinese pronunciation "Mankuk," just as "Mongol" and "Mughal" were derived from it. In the field of linguistic evolution, there are countless parallels to this.

So Gog and Magog are the Mongols. This nation, inhabiting northeastern Asia from the most ancient times, was a large, attacking nation that would raid China at times, and at other times through the Darial Pass of the Caucasus into Armenia, northern Iran, and elsewhere. At another time—after the building of the barrier—they raided Northern Europe and were called Scythians (سيت) by them. Among them was the nation that attacked Rome, leading to the fall of the Roman Empire in that instance. It was also previously mentioned that what is derived from the books of the Old Testament is that this corrupting nation was from the inhabitants of the far north.

This is the sum of what we have summarized from his discourse. Although it is not free from some objections in certain aspects, it is more clearly applicable to the verses and closer to acceptance than others.

**G -** Among what has been said regarding Dhu al-Qarnayn is what I heard from some of my teachers: that he was from the people of some cycles (أدوار) preceding this human cycle. This is strange, and perhaps it was to validate what has been reported in the traditions regarding the wondrous states of Dhu al-Qarnayn and the strange events that occurred from him, such as his dying and coming to life time after time, his being raised to heaven and descending to earth, the clouds being made subservient to him to carry him to the West and East, and the subservience of light, darkness, thunder, and lightning to him. It is well known that the history of this cycle preserves nothing of that, so it must have been in some preceding cycles. This view was based on an excessive good opinion of those traditions.

4 - Exegetes and historians have delved deeply into the research surrounding the story and have spoken extensively on its various aspects. Most of them agree that Gog and Magog are a large nation in Northern Asia. A group of them applied what the Quran informed regarding their emergence at the end of time and their corruption in the land to the Mongol (Tatar) invasion in the first half of the seventh century AH upon Western Asia. They were excessive in destroying crops and progeny by demolishing cities, annihilating souls, looting wealth, and committing atrocities that no predecessor had surpassed.

They first subjugated China, then marched to Turkestan, Iran, Iraq, the Levant, and the Caucasus into Asia Minor. They annihilated everything that resisted them of cities, towns, and fortresses, such as Samarkand, Bukhara, Khwarazm, Merv, Nishapur, Ray, and others. A city would wake up with thousands of souls in it and by evening not a single person would remain to light a fire, nor a stone upon a stone of its buildings.

Then they returned to their lands, then came back and attacked the Russians, destroyed the people of Poland and the lands of Hungary, and attacked the Romans, forcing them to pay tribute—all in atrocities whose description would be lengthy.

However, they neglected to research the matter of the barrier from the perspective of their emergence from it and solving its problem. For the saying of the Exalted: **فَمَا اسْطاعُوا أَنْ يَظْهَرُوهُ وَمَا اسْتَطاعُوا لَهُ نَقْباً قالَ هذا رَحْمَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّي فَإِذا جاءَ وَعْدُ رَبِّي جَعَلَهُ دَكَّاءَ وَكانَ وَعْدُ رَبِّي حَقًّا وَتَرَكْنا بَعْضَهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَمُوجُ فِي بَعْضٍ** "So they could not scale it, nor could they dig a hole in it. He said, ‘This is a mercy from my Lord. But when the promise of my Lord comes to pass, He will level it, and the promise of my Lord is true.’ On that day We shall leave them to surge against one another..." (18:97-99), its apparent meaning—according to their interpretation—is that this corrupting nation is imprisoned behind it, with no exit to the rest of the earth as long as it remains standing, until when the promise of Allah the Glorified comes, He will make it leveled or collapsed, and they will emerge from it to the people and spread corruption and evil.

Based on this, they ought to have established the description of this barrier (السد). If the mentioned nation refers to the Tatars, who marched from northern China to Iran, Iraq, the Levant, and the Caucasus, and then into Asia Minor, then where was this barrier described in the Quran—which they supposedly trampled over before emerging into these lands and turning them upside down?

And if it was not the Tatars or any other invading nations throughout history, then where is this barrier constructed of iron (السد المشيد بالحديد)? Its description is such that it has confined a massive nation for thousands of years, preventing them from attacking the rest of the world’s regions, with no exit to the rest of the world except through the barrier struck before them. Today, the various regions of the earth have become interconnected via land, sea, and air routes. No natural barrier—be it a mountain or a sea—nor any man-made one—be it a dam, a wall, or a trench—prevents one nation from reaching another. What meaning, then, remains for a people being blocked from the world by a barrier between two mountains, regardless of how it is described or how its existence is posited?

What I hold regarding the resolution of this problem—and Allah knows best—is that His saying: **دَكَّاءَ** (leveled) is derived from **الدك** (leveling/humbling) in the sense of "humiliation" or "insignificance" (الذلة). It is stated in *Lisan al-Arab*: "A leveled mountain (جبل دك) is one that is humbled (ذليل)." End quote. The meaning of making the barrier "leveled" (دكاء) is making it insignificant—such that its presence is no longer reckoned with, nor is it of any use, due to the expansion of communication routes and the diversification of means of movement and transport by land, sea, and air.

Thus, the reality of this promise is a promise of the advancement of human society in its civilization (مدنيته), and the drawing together of its various nations to a point where no barrier can block them, nor any wall encompass them to prevent movement from any corner of the earth to another, nor stop them from attacking or marching upon whichever people they wish.

This meaning is supported by the context of the saying of the Exalted in another place where He mentions the onslaught of Gog and Magog:

**حَتَّى إِذَا فُتِحَتْ يَأْجُوجُ وَمَأْجُوجُ وَهُمْ مِنْ كُلِّ حَدَبٍ يَنْسِلُونَ**
"Until when Gog and Magog are let loose, and they gently flow down from every tier." (21:96)

Here, He expressed it as the "opening" (فتح) of Gog and Magog and did not mention the barrier.

Furthermore, **الدك** (leveling) has another meaning, which is "burying with earth" (الدفن بالتراب). It is stated in *al-Sihah*: "I leveled the well (دككت الركي)—meaning I buried it with earth." End quote. It has yet another meaning: a mountain becoming a mound of clay. It is stated in *al-Sihah*: "The mountains were leveled (تدكدكت الجبال)—meaning they became mounds of clay; the singular is *dakka’* (دكاء)." End quote. Thus, it is possible to assume that the barrier is among the structures of ancient eras that have become buried under the earth due to violent winds, or submerged by the shifting of seas or the expansion of some of them, as established by geological research (الأبحاث الجيولوجية). By this, the problem is resolved; however, the previous explanation is more plausible, and Allah knows best.
